coreIdea: a^(p−1) ≡ 1 (mod p) when p is prime and a is not divisible by p — the foundation of modern cryptography.
In a party of n guests each shaking hands once with everyone else, the total handshakes = n(n−1)/2.
When remainders from different divisions are compatible, they together pinpoint exactly one number modulo the product.
On average, collecting n coupons requires about n × H_n draws — the last few are always the hardest.
An ancient method to find GCD(a,b) efficiently by repeated remainders — still the backbone of modern cryptography.
The number x satisfying a × x ≡ 1 (mod m) exists iff gcd(a,m)=1 — the key to RSA decryption.
Computing a^n in O(log n) multiplications instead of O(n) by exploiting binary representation — used in every secure HTTPS connection.
